1:0.rendering: I said in my heart, "Come now, I will test you with pleasure. So enjoy yourself." But look, this too was vapour.
1:0.note: The Hebrew word for "pleasure" here echoes the garden's delights in Genesis, yet the Preacher finds it as fleeting as breath.

1:1.rendering: I said of laughter, "It is madness," and of pleasure, "What does it accomplish?"
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "madness" here echoes the book's opening verdict that "all is vapour" under the sun, exposing even royal merriment as empty wind.

1:2.rendering: I searched in my heart to cheer my flesh with wine, while guiding my heart with wisdom, and to lay hold on folly, until I could see what was good for the children of humanity under the heavens during the few days of their lives.
1:2.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here echoes the king's quest in 1 Kings 3 for wisdom, but now it's twisted into an experiment mixing pleasure and insight.

1:3.rendering: I built myself houses, I planted vineyards for myself.
1:3.note: The Hebrew piles up "for myself" five times in verses 4 through 8, exposing the self-centered quest for meaning under the sun.

1:4.rendering: I made myself gardens and parks, and I planted in them every kind of fruit tree.
1:4.note: The Hebrew word for "parks" (pardes) is a Persian loanword, evoking the lush royal gardens of ancient empires that the Preacher imitated in his quest for meaning.

1:5.rendering: I made pools of water for myself, to water from them the forest sprouting with trees.
1:5.note: The Hebrew word for "pools" echoes the reservoirs of Egypt and the lush gardens of ancient kings, exposing how Solomon's building spree mimicked the very empires he was meant to surpass.

1:6.rendering: I bought male and female slaves, and I had slaves born in my house. I also had great possessions of herds and flocks, more than all who were in Jerusalem before me.
1:6.note: The Hebrew word for "acquired" here echoes God's own language of "getting" in Genesis 4:1, exposing how the quest for control over life and legacy always slips through human fingers.

1:7.rendering: I piled up silver and gold for myself, the treasure of kings and provinces. I got myself male and female singers, and the delights of the sons of man: many concubines.
1:7.note: The Hebrew word for "delights" echoes the pleasure garden of Eden, but here it twists into a self-made paradise of excess.

1:8.rendering: So I became greater than all who were before me in Jerusalem. And my wisdom remained with me.
1:8.note: The Hebrew word for "greater" echoes the serpent's promise in Genesis 3:5 to be "like God," exposing the futility of Solomon-like wisdom pursued apart from fear of the Lord.

1:9.rendering: I denied my eyes nothing they desired. I refused my heart no pleasure. My heart took delight in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.
1:9.note: The Hebrew word for "delight" here echoes the repeated "toil" language, showing how the Preacher chased joy through endless work only to find it fleeting.

1:10.rendering: Then I looked at all the works my hands had done and at the toil I had labored to achieve, and look, everything was vapour, a chasing after wind. There was no lasting profit under the sun.
1:10.note: The Hebrew word hevel, meaning vapour or breath, appears here like a puff of smoke that vanishes, echoing the book's opening cry in 1:2.

1:11.rendering: Then I turned to see wisdom and madness and folly, for what can the man do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.
1:11.note: The Hebrew word for "turned" echoes the book's theme of endless cycles, hinting that even the quest for wisdom loops back on itself.

1:12.rendering: I saw that wisdom excels folly as light excels darkness.
1:12.note: The Hebrew wordplay between "light" and "eyes" (both from the same root) hints that wisdom is what truly opens our eyes in a dark world.

1:13.rendering: The wise person has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also saw that one fate happens to them all.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "eyes in his head" plays on the idea of sight versus blindness, yet death levels both paths.

1:14.rendering: Then I said in my heart, "What happens to the fool will happen to me also. Why then have I been so exceedingly wise?" And I said in my heart that this also is vapour.
1:14.note: The word "vapour" echoes the book's opening poem, showing even wisdom's edge over folly dissolves like breath.

1:15.rendering: For the wise person dies just like the fool, and so the memory of both will be forgotten in the days to come. How then does the wise die like the fool?
1:15.note: The Hebrew word for "memory" here echoes the name of the teacher himself, Qohelet, hinting that even his own legacy will vanish.

1:16.rendering: So I hated life, because what is done under the sun seemed evil to me. Everything is vapour, a chasing after wind.
1:16.note: The Hebrew word for "hated" echoes God's rejection of evil in the prophets, showing the teacher's disgust mirrors divine displeasure at a broken world.

1:17.rendering: I hated all my toil that I toiled under the sun, which I am leaving to the one who comes after me.
1:17.note: The Hebrew word for "hated" echoes the same strong emotion used for rejecting idols, revealing how even fruitful work can feel pointless when death steals the legacy.

1:18.rendering: And who knows whether he will be a wise person or a fool? Yet he will rule over all the toil that I toiled at and that I became wise under the sun. This also is vapour.
1:18.note: The Hebrew word for "who knows" echoes the teacher's earlier despair in 3:21 and 6:12, underscoring our inability to control what comes after us.

1:19.rendering: So I turned to let my heart despair over all the toil that I had toiled under the sun.
1:19.note: The Hebrew word for "despair" carries the idea of abandoning hope, a stark pivot after the king's exhaustive search for meaning in work.

1:20.rendering: For there is a man whose toil is with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, yet he must leave his portion to a man who has not toiled for it. This also is vapour and a chasing after wind.
1:20.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" echoes the inheritance language of the Promised Land, exposing how death turns even wise labor into something fleeting.

1:21.rendering: For what does a person have from all his toil and the striving of his heart with which he toils under the sun?
1:21.note: The word for "striving" echoes the "chasing after wind" motif that threads through the book, exposing how our driven labor often leaves us grasping at nothing.

1:22.rendering: For all his days are pain and his work is vexation. Even at night his heart does not rest. This also is vapour.
1:22.note: The Hebrew word "ra'yon" for "vexation" echoes the "chasing after wind" motif, showing how toil leaves the heart restless even in sleep.

1:23.rendering: There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find satisfaction in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God.
1:23.note: Qoheleth's Hebrew for "find satisfaction" literally means "cause his soul to see good," an ancient way of saying enjoy life with your whole being.

1:24.rendering: For who can eat or enjoy himself apart from him?
1:24.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "eat" and "see good" echoes the garden of Eden, where true enjoyment was meant to flow directly from relationship with the Creator.

1:25.rendering: For to the one who is good in his sight, God gives wisdom and knowledge and joy, but to the sinner he gives the task of gathering and collecting only to hand it over to the one who is good in God's sight. This too is vapour and a chasing after wind.
1:25.note: The word for "good in his sight" echoes God's delight in creation, hinting that true enjoyment is a divine gift rather than a human achievement.

0:0.rendering: The words of Qohelet, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
0:0.note: Qohelet means "the assembler" or "the preacher," a title that echoes the gathering of Israel at Solomon's dedication of the temple.

0:1.rendering: Utter vapour, says the Teacher, utter vapour. Everything is vapour.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word hevel, repeated five times here, means not just "vanity" but literally vapour or breath, the same image used for fleeting human life in Psalm 39 and 144.

0:2.rendering: What does a person gain from all the toil at which he labors under the sun?
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" (yitron) evokes surplus or lasting profit, yet the verse frames human labor as a question that echoes through the book.

0:3.rendering: A generation goes and a generation comes, but the earth stands forever.
0:3.note: The Hebrew word for "stands" echoes the creation language of Genesis 1, where the land emerges to stay put while human life cycles like breath.

0:4.rendering: The sun rises and the sun sets, then it hurries back to the place where it rises again.
0:4.note: This verse echoes the ancient Near Eastern view of the sun's tireless circuit, a vivid image of endless repetition that sets the tone for Qoheleth's meditation on life's cycles.

0:5.rendering: The wind goes to the south, and circles to the north. It circles and circles on its rounds, and the wind returns on its circuits.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "wind" is the same as "spirit" or "breath," creating a wordplay that hints at the restless, unseen forces shaping life under the sun.

0:6.rendering: All the streams flow to the sea, yet the sea is never full. To the place where the streams flow, there they return again.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "streams" is the same as "rivers" in Genesis 2:10, quietly echoing the original garden's life-giving waters now caught in this weary cycle.

0:7.rendering: All things are wearisome, more than one can say. The eye is never satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "wearisome" (yĕgĕʿim) carries the sense of exhausting toil, echoing the cursed ground in Genesis 3:17-19 that now drains human effort.

0:8.rendering: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.
0:8.note: This Hebrew phrase plays on the word for "under the sun" to paint life as a repetitive cycle observed from a purely earthly vantage point.

0:9.rendering: Is there anything of which one might say, "See this, it is new"? It has already been, in the ages before us.
0:9.note: The Teacher's rhetorical question exposes our illusion of originality, echoing how every generation thinks its discoveries are unprecedented.

0:10.rendering: There is no remembrance of the people who came before us, and of those who will come after us, there will be no remembrance by those who come after them.
0:10.note: The Hebrew word for "remembrance" echoes the urgent refrain of Genesis, where names and stories are meant to endure, yet here the Teacher watches them dissolve like vapour.

0:11.rendering: I, the Teacher, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "Teacher" is Qoheleth, a title that echoes the gathered assembly or congregation, hinting this is wisdom spoken for the community.

0:12.rendering: I gave my heart to seek and to explore by wisdom everything that is done under heaven. It is a miserable business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.
0:12.note: The phrase "miserable business" renders the Hebrew "evil task," an ancient Near Eastern expression for the wearisome labor of searching that God has assigned humanity.

0:13.rendering: I have seen all the deeds that are done under the sun, and look, all of them are vapour, and a chasing after wind.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word hevel, often translated "vanity," literally means "vapour" or "breath," evoking something fleeting and ungraspable.

0:14.rendering: What is crooked cannot be straightened, and what is lacking cannot be counted.
0:14.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "straightened" and "lacking" echoes the futility of human toil in a broken creation, inviting slow meditation on limits we cannot fix.

0:15.rendering: I spoke with my own heart, saying, "Look, I have become great and increased in wisdom beyond all who were before me over Jerusalem, and my heart has seen a wealth of wisdom and knowledge."
0:15.note: The Hebrew word for "heart" here carries the ancient sense of the seat of intellect and will, showing the Preacher turning inward to test wisdom's limits.

0:16.rendering: I gave my mind to know wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly. I understood that this too is a chasing after wind.
0:16.note: The Hebrew pairs "wisdom and knowledge" with "madness and folly" in a deliberate echo of the book's opening obsession with opposites, showing even the quest for insight can become futile grasping.

0:17.rendering: For with great wisdom comes great frustration, and the more knowledge, the more sorrow.
0:17.note: The Hebrew wordplay links wisdom (hokmah) directly to vexation (ka'as), showing that deeper insight into life's futility only intensifies grief.

2:0.rendering: There is a time for everything, and a season for every matter under heaven.
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here is et, which carries the sense of an appointed or fitting moment rather than just the ticking clock, echoing the ordered rhythm of creation in Genesis 1 where God separates and appoints times and purposes. This poem in Ecclesiastes invites us to embrace the beautiful, sometimes painful, seasons of human life as part of the unified story where the Creator weaves all things toward their ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, who entered our time to redeem it.

2:1.rendering: A time to give birth, and a time to die. A time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted.
2:1.note: The Hebrew word 'et' (time) repeats like a steady drumbeat, framing all of human life within God's appointed rhythms, much like the ordered cycles of creation in Genesis 1. This poem echoes the image-of-God mandate to 'be fruitful and multiply' and to cultivate the ground, yet holds both life and death, planting and uprooting, in tension, pointing forward to the one who would enter our time-bound story to bring new creation out of death.

2:2.rendering: A time to tear down and a time to build up, a time to kill and a time to heal.
2:2.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here, et, carries a sense of the right or appointed moment, echoing the rhythms of creation and covenant in Genesis where God both tears down chaos to build order and brings life out of death. This verse sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' meditation on the seasons of human existence under the sun, pointing forward to Jesus who embodies both tearing down the old order in his death and building up new creation in his resurrection.

2:3.rendering: A time to weep, and a time to laugh, a time to mourn, and a time to dance.
2:3.note: This verse sits in the poem's rhythmic catalog of 'times' that together portray the full range of human experience under the sun, echoing the creation pattern of order amid apparent chaos, much like the alternating seasons of joy and sorrow that mark Israel's story from exodus joy to exile lament and back again.

2:4.rendering: a time to throw stones away, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing.
2:4.note: The Hebrew word for "stones" (evenim) echoes the story of Israel's ancestors building altars and memorials with stones to mark moments when heaven and earth overlapped. This verse sits in the poem's rhythm of opposites, inviting us to notice the wisdom of discerning the right moment in the human story, a wisdom that finds its ultimate expression in Jesus who knew when to gather his friends close and when to release them into the world.

2:5.rendering: A time to search and a time to give up searching, a time to keep and a time to throw away.
2:5.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here (baqash) echoes throughout the book as humanity's restless quest for meaning under the sun, while "give up" carries the sense of letting go or losing something precious. This pairs with the next line's keep-and-throw-away rhythm, inviting us into the wisdom of knowing when to hold tightly to God's gifts and when to release them, a tension that finds its ultimate resolution in Jesus who both seeks the lost and teaches us to lose our lives for his sake.

2:6.rendering: A time to tear and a time to mend, a time to be silent and a time to speak.
2:6.note: The Hebrew word for “tear” (qaraʿ) echoes the dramatic rending of garments in grief or repentance throughout the Old Testament, from Joshua and David to the prophets. In the unified story, this pairs with the later moment when the temple veil is torn from top to bottom at Jesus’ death, signaling the end of one era of separation and the beginning of new access between heaven and earth.

2:7.rendering: a time to love and a time to hate, a time for war and a time for peace.
2:7.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here (et) echoes throughout the poem, painting seasons as part of the ordered rhythm of God's good world, yet one that feels frustratingly out of our control. This pairs love with hate and war with peace in ways that startle us, reminding us that even the darkest human realities have their place under the sun, until the Prince of Peace arrives to finally reorder all things.

2:8.rendering: What advantage does the worker have in exchange for the work he does?
2:8.note: This question echoes the word "profit" or "advantage" (yitron in Hebrew) that runs like a refrain through Ecclesiastes, probing whether human toil under the sun adds up to anything lasting. It sits at the hinge of the famous poem on seasons, inviting us to wonder if our labor fits into a larger pattern only the Creator fully sees, a thread that later finds its answer in the New Testament vision of work offered to the Lord.

2:9.rendering: I have seen the task that God has given to the children of humanity to be busy with.
2:9.note: The word translated "task" here is the same Hebrew word for "business" or "occupation" that appears throughout Ecclesiastes, echoing the frustration of humanity's endless toil under the sun. This verse sits at the heart of the poem on seasons, showing how God has woven time and labor into the human experience, a pattern that finds its deeper resolution in Jesus who invites us to rest from our striving.

2:10.rendering: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into their hearts, yet so that no one can find out the work that God has done from beginning to end.
2:10.note: The Hebrew word 'olam' here, often translated 'eternity,' carries the sense of hidden, distant time that stretches beyond our grasp. This verse sits at the center of Ecclesiastes' poem about seasons, showing how God has wired humans with an awareness of the full sweep of time while keeping the bigger picture veiled, a theme that echoes through Israel's story of exile and return, and finds its resolution in Jesus who reveals the end from the beginning.

2:11.rendering: I know that there is nothing better for them than to be glad and to do good in their lives.
2:11.note: The key Hebrew word here for "good" (tov) echoes God's repeated declaration in Genesis 1 that creation is "good," inviting us to participate in that original goodness through simple joy and moral action even under the sun. This verse sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' wisdom, showing how the Preacher finds fleeting but real meaning in the rhythms of time by embracing what God has given, a thread that later finds its full expression in Jesus' invitation to abundant life amid the brokenness of our world.

2:12.rendering: And also that everyone should eat and drink and find good in all their toil. This is a gift from God.
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "gift" here is the same one used for the divine favor that rests on humanity in the creation story. Amid the poem's rhythmic cycles of time in Ecclesiastes 3, this verse echoes Genesis 1-2, where God gifts humanity the good work of stewarding creation and enjoying its fruit, a thread of divine generosity that runs through the whole biblical story and finds its fullest expression in Jesus.

2:13.rendering: I know that everything God does will last forever. Nothing can be added to it, and nothing can be taken away from it. God has done this so that people will stand in awe before him.
2:13.note: The word for "forever" here is olam, and the Preacher is playing with the idea that God's works stand outside the endless cycles of toil and death described in the rest of the book. This verse quietly echoes the creation account where God declares his work "very good" and sets boundaries that cannot be undone, pointing forward to the unified biblical theme that the Creator is faithfully stitching together a story that will not unravel, ultimately revealed in Jesus as the one through whom all things hold together.

2:14.rendering: Whatever has been, it is already; and whatever is going to be, it was already. And God seeks out what has been pursued.
2:14.note: The Hebrew word for "seeks out" (baqash) carries the sense of God carefully investigating or even "driving after" what has been driven away, a subtle echo of his pursuit of humanity after the exile-like scattering of Genesis 3. This verse sits at the center of Ecclesiastes' poem on times and seasons, revealing how the Teacher sees the relentless cycles of life not as meaningless repetition but as something God himself is actively weaving into his unified story that will one day find its resolution in Jesus, the one who steps into time to redeem what was lost.

2:15.rendering: I saw something else under the sun: in the place of justice there was wickedness, and in the place of righteousness there was wickedness.
2:15.note: The Teacher stares straight at the twisted places where human systems meant to uphold justice and righteousness have instead become engines of oppression, a pattern that echoes the fractured image-of-God rule we see from Genesis 3 onward. This observation sits at the heart of Ecclesiastes' wisdom, holding the tension between the good order God designed and the crooked reality we experience until the Messiah comes to set all things right.

2:16.rendering: I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every matter and for every deed.
2:16.note: The Hebrew word for "time" here, et, echoes the poem's refrain from earlier in the chapter, creating a subtle wordplay that ties human activity to divine order. This verse holds the tension between the apparent randomness of life under the sun and the ancient hope of a coming judge, a thread that weaves through Israel's story and finds its surprising fulfillment when Jesus steps into history as the one appointed to judge the living and the dead.

2:17.rendering: I said in my heart concerning the sons of man, that God is testing them, and that they might see they are but animals.
2:17.note: The Hebrew word for "animals" here is behemah, the same term used in Genesis 1 for the livestock and wild beasts God made on day six, right before forming humans as his image. Qohelet stares at the shared fate of death that humans and animals have in common, and he wonders if this is God's way of exposing our illusion of superiority, a thread that runs from creation's goodness through the curse of Genesis 3 and finds its ultimate resolution when the Son of Man comes as the true image who shares our flesh yet defeats death for us all.

2:18.rendering: For the fate of the sons of man and the fate of the beast is one fate. As one dies, so dies the other. They all have one breath. Man has no advantage over the beast, for all is vapour.
2:18.note: The Hebrew word for "fate" (miqreh) here plays on the idea of "happening" or "meeting," echoing the Teacher's earlier observation that death is the great equalizer that levels every human project, much like how the flood in Genesis 6-9 showed all flesh sharing the same breath and returning to dust.

2:19.rendering: All go to one place. All are from the dust, and all return to dust.
2:19.note: The Teacher echoes Genesis 3:19 word for word, where humanity's rebellion leads to the ground (adamah) reclaiming the human (adam). This shared fate of dust-to-dust binds all creatures in the unified story of creation, curse, and mortality, a thread that finds its surprising resolution when the last Adam rises from the dust, conquering the grave.

2:20.rendering: Who knows whether the spirit of the sons of man goes upward and the spirit of the animal goes down into the earth?
2:20.note: The Hebrew word ruach, meaning both breath and spirit, creates a haunting wordplay here as Qohelet stares at the shared fate of humans and animals under the sun, their life-breath returning to dust. This question echoes Genesis 2-3 and the cursed ground of exile, pressing us to wonder if there is any lasting distinction between image-bearers and beasts, a tension the unified biblical story will resolve when Jesus breathes new resurrection life into humanity.

2:21.rendering: So I saw that there is nothing better for a person than to rejoice in their work, because that is their portion. For who can bring them to see what will be after them?
2:21.note: The Hebrew word for "portion" (cheleq) echoes the language of Israel's inheritance in the land, yet here the Preacher locates our true share right in the work and joy of the present moment under the sun. This verse sits at the climax of the poem on time in chapter 3, inviting us to embrace the gift of today while the larger biblical story quietly points forward to the day when the curse on toil is lifted and our labor participates in God's new creation.
